findings.description: Complex table headers with merged cells were split and flattened, significantly altering the visual structure.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, top-level header cells for dates (e.g., '2/14/2014') and report numbers span across multiple sub-columns. In the HTML, these merged header cells have been split. The HTML creates a single, extremely tall header row where each cell contains a concatenation of all its parent header text (e.g., '2/14/2014 Report # AC04986 Lab AC04987 Dissolved AC04993 Value').

findings.description: Text spanning multiple columns in data rows is duplicated instead of merged.
findings.evidence: In the PDF rows for 'Al - Dissolved Aluminum' and 'As Dissolved Arsenic', the text notes in the final two columns ('no NC standards for' and 'dissolved metals') span across the 'Applicable Water Quality Standard' and 'Basis for Standard' columns. The HTML splits these cells and duplicates the text into both columns.

findings.description: Floating text spanning multiple empty cells in the PDF was split and duplicated into distinct table cells in the HTML.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the text 'no NC standards for dissolved metals' spans the final two columns next to the first few 'Dissolved' rows. In the HTML, this text is split across two rows and duplicated across the two columns: the row for 'Al - Dissolved Aluminum' contains 'no NC standards for' in both the last two columns, and the row for 'As Dissolved Arsenic' contains 'dissolved metals' in both columns.
